title: SAT Diagnostic 1 (Baseline)
description: A balanced baseline diagnostic across the main SAT Reading & Writing and Math topics. Every question is topic-tagged so the results screen shows a weak-area breakdown. Use it to set a starting point toward the 1400 goal.

questions.q: The scientist's explanation was so ______ that even people with no background in the field grasped the concept at once.
questions.explanation: If non-experts understood it immediately, the explanation was clear — 'lucid.'

questions.q: Once ______ by critics as clumsy and derivative, the author's debut novel is now regarded as a landmark of modern fiction.
questions.explanation: The contrast with 'now regarded as a landmark' signals earlier rejection — 'dismissed.'

questions.q:
Coral reefs support roughly a quarter of all marine species, yet they cover less than one percent of the ocean floor. When ocean temperatures rise, corals expel the algae that give them color and much of their energy, an event called bleaching. Prolonged bleaching can kill a reef, and with it the vast web of life that depends on it.

Which choice best states the main idea of the text?
choices: Coral reefs cover less than one percent of the ocean floor.
Rising ocean temperatures threaten reefs and the many species that depend on them.
Bleaching causes corals to lose their color.
Most marine species live far from coral reefs.
questions.explanation: The passage builds to the danger warming poses to reefs and dependent species; the other choices are details or contradictions.

questions.q:
A team of biologists hypothesized that birds in cities sing at a higher pitch than their rural relatives in order to be heard over low-frequency traffic noise.

Which finding, if true, would most directly support the biologists' hypothesis?
choices: Rural birds defend larger territories than city birds.
In noisy areas, city birds that sing at higher pitches attract mates more successfully than those that sing at lower pitches.
Traffic noise in cities has increased over the past decade.
City birds and rural birds eat similar diets.
questions.explanation: Only this links higher pitch to an advantage amid noise, directly supporting the hypothesis.

questions.q:
In a survey of commuters, satisfaction with public transit was closely tied to reliability. Among riders who said their train was 'almost always on time,' 82 percent reported being satisfied; among those who said their train was 'frequently delayed,' only 29 percent reported satisfaction.

Which choice best uses the data to support the claim that reliability shapes rider satisfaction?
choices: Most commuters ride the train every day.
Satisfaction was far higher among riders with reliable trains (82 percent) than among those with frequently delayed trains (29 percent).
Fewer than one-third of all commuters were satisfied with transit.
Transit agencies find reliability difficult to guarantee.
questions.explanation: The 82%-vs-29% contrast is the data that directly ties reliability to satisfaction.

questions.q: For years the abandoned factory was considered an eyesore. But when developers converted it into loft apartments and studios, property values on the surrounding blocks climbed sharply, and new cafes and shops soon opened nearby. The neighborhood's transformation suggests that ______
choices: abandoned buildings should always be demolished.
repurposing an old structure can spark broader economic revival.
loft apartments are more profitable than shops.
the factory should have been left closed.
questions.explanation: Reuse of the factory preceded rising values and new businesses, implying reuse can drive revival.

questions.q:
Many people assume goosebumps serve no purpose in humans. In fact, the reflex is a leftover from our evolutionary past: in furry mammals, the same muscle contraction raises the coat to trap heat or make the animal look larger. In humans, with far less body hair, the response persists even though its original benefit has mostly vanished.

Which choice best describes the main purpose of the text?
choices: To argue that goosebumps are harmful.
To explain that a seemingly pointless human reflex is an evolutionary holdover.
To compare humans with other primates.
To describe in detail how muscles contract.
questions.explanation: The text reframes a 'pointless' reflex as an evolutionary remnant — that is its purpose.

questions.q:
Text 1: Standardized tests provide a common yardstick, letting colleges compare applicants from thousands of different high schools on equal terms.

Text 2: Because scores correlate strongly with family income, tests often measure a student's resources more than their potential, disadvantaging capable applicants from under-resourced schools.

Based on the texts, how would the author of Text 2 most likely respond to the 'common yardstick' argument in Text 1?
choices: By agreeing that tests are the fairest available measure.
By arguing that the yardstick is skewed by factors like income and therefore is not truly fair.
By suggesting colleges ignore high school records entirely.
By claiming the tests are too easy to be useful.
questions.explanation: Text 2's income argument directly undercuts Text 1's claim that the yardstick is fair.

questions.q: After months of painstaking restoration ______ the antique clock finally chimed again.
questions.explanation: An introductory phrase must be set off from the main clause with a comma; a semicolon or colon would be incorrect here.

questions.q: The recipe depends on three things that beginning bakers often overlook ______ patience, precise measurement, and high-quality butter.
questions.explanation: A colon correctly introduces the list after the independent clause.

questions.q: The array of colorful antique marbles ______ displayed in a glass case near the museum entrance.
questions.explanation: The singular subject 'array' takes 'is'; 'of colorful antique marbles' is a modifying phrase, not the subject.

questions.q: By the time the rescue team reached the ridge, the stranded hikers ______ a signal fire to mark their location.
questions.explanation: An action completed before another past event takes the past perfect: 'had built.'

questions.q: The committee announced ______ final decision only after weeks of heated debate.
questions.explanation: 'Committee' is singular, and the possessive is 'its' (no apostrophe).

questions.q: Rushing to catch the last train, ______
choices: Maria barely made it through the closing doors.
the doors closed behind the conductor.
questions.explanation: The opening modifier describes the person rushing, so 'Maria' must follow the comma.

questions.q: The prototype passed every laboratory test. ______, it failed almost immediately under real-world conditions.
questions.explanation: The real-world failure contrasts with the lab success, so a contrast transition ('However') fits.

questions.q: Ticket sales had declined for three straight seasons. ______, the theater's board voted to shorten the upcoming schedule.
questions.explanation: The board's decision is a result of falling sales, so a cause-effect transition ('Consequently') is needed.

questions.q:
A student wants to emphasize the extraordinary age of the bristlecone pine. Notes: bristlecone pines grow in the western United States; some are more than 4,800 years old; they grow very slowly; their wood is unusually dense.

Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?
choices: Bristlecone pines grow in the western United States.
Some bristlecone pines are more than 4,800 years old.
The wood of the bristlecone pine is unusually dense.
questions.explanation: Only the age fact serves the goal of emphasizing how old the trees are.

questions.q: Far from being ______, the senator's remarks were carefully calculated to win over undecided voters.
questions.explanation: 'Far from' sets up the opposite of 'carefully calculated' — unplanned, or 'impromptu.'

questions.q:
Historians once portrayed the Silk Road as a single highway of commerce. Newer scholarship, however, describes it as a sprawling network of overlapping routes along which not only goods but also religions, technologies, and even diseases traveled.

Which choice best states the main idea of the text?
choices: The Silk Road was a single, clearly defined road.
Recent scholarship recasts the Silk Road as a broad network that carried far more than goods.
The Silk Road was used mainly to spread diseases.
Historians agree on very little about the Silk Road.
questions.explanation: The passage revises the 'single highway' view into a wide network moving goods and ideas alike.

questions.q: The Hubble Space Telescope ______ has transformed our understanding of the cosmos, continues to send back stunning images decades after its launch.
questions.explanation: The nonrestrictive clause 'which has transformed...cosmos' is opened with a comma before 'which' and closed with the comma before 'continues.'

questions.q: If 5x - 4 = 26, what is the value of x?
A line passes through the points (1, 2) and (4, 11). What is the slope of the line?
If 2x + y = 13 and x - y = 2, what is the value of x?
questions.explanation: Adding the equations gives 3x = 15, so x = 5.

questions.q: If 3x + 2 > 14, which of the following could be the value of x?
questions.explanation: 3x > 12 means x > 4, so 4.5 works (4 is not greater than 4).

questions.q: What is 15% of 80?
If 4 pens cost $6.00, how much do 10 pens cost at the same rate?
Which expression is equivalent to (2x^3)(3x^4)?
questions.explanation: Multiply coefficients (2 x 3 = 6) and add exponents (3 + 4 = 7): 6x^7.

questions.q: What is the sum of the solutions to x^2 - 7x + 12 = 0?
questions.explanation: The equation factors as (x - 3)(x - 4) = 0; the solutions 3 and 4 sum to 7.

questions.q: The graph of f(x) = x^2 - 6x + 5 is a parabola. What is the x-coordinate of its vertex?
questions.explanation: The vertex x-coordinate is -b/(2a) = 6/2 = 3.

questions.q: If g(x) = 3x^2 - x, what is the value of g(2)?
A right triangle has legs of length 5 and 12. What is the length of its hypotenuse?
questions.explanation: The square root of (25 + 144) is the square root of 169, which is 13.

questions.q: What is the area of a circle with radius 3? (Leave the answer in terms of pi.)
questions.explanation: Area = pi x r^2 = pi x 9 = 9 pi.

questions.q: Two interior angles of a triangle measure 40 degrees and 75 degrees. What is the measure of the third angle?
questions.explanation: Angles of a triangle sum to 180: 180 - 40 - 75 = 65 degrees.

questions.q: In a right triangle, the side opposite angle A has length 3 and the hypotenuse has length 5. What is sin A?
questions.explanation: sin = opposite / hypotenuse = 3/5.

questions.q: The mean (average) of the four numbers 4, 8, 10, and x is 8. What is the value of x?
questions.explanation: The four numbers must total 32; 4 + 8 + 10 = 22, so x = 10.

questions.q: What is the median of the data set 3, 7, 9, 12, 15?
questions.explanation: With five values in order, the middle value is 9.

questions.q: A bag contains 3 red, 5 green, and 2 blue marbles. If one marble is drawn at random, what is the probability that it is green? (Enter a decimal or fraction.)
questions.explanation: 5 green out of 10 total = 5/10 = 1/2 = 0.5. (Equivalent forms are accepted.)

questions.q: A shirt originally priced at $40 is discounted by 25%. What is the sale price, in dollars?
A taxi charges a $3 base fare plus $2 per mile. What is the total cost, in dollars, of an 8-mile ride?
A colony of 200 bacteria doubles every hour. How many bacteria are there after 3 hours?
